topic: insurance company rant — denied periodontal claim

turns.content: I need to vent for a moment, if you do not mind. I just got off the phone with an insurance company and I am genuinely beside myself.
Go right ahead — sounds like it was a rough call. What happened?

I have a patient, jan, a lovely woman in her sixties. Moderate to severe periodontitis. Bone loss clearly visible on the radiographs. I recommended scaling and root planing — this is textbook, first-line treatment. The insurance company denied the claim. Denied it. They said it was not medically necessary.

That's incredibly frustrating, especially for a treatment that's considered standard of care. Did they give a specific reason beyond "not medically necessary"? Sometimes insurance companies use narrow criteria — like requiring a certain pocket depth threshold or specific documentation before they'll approve.

They said the pocket depths were insufficient. The patient has four-millimeter and five-millimeter pockets with bleeding on probing and radiographic bone loss. By every clinical guideline in existence, this patient needs treatment. But some reviewer sitting in an office somewhere, who has never examined this woman, decided it is not necessary.

That's a common and infuriating pattern. A lot of dental insurance reviewers use rigid numerical cutoffs — some won't approve SRP unless pockets are 5mm or deeper, ignoring the full clinical picture like bleeding on probing, attachment loss, and bone loss. The fact that they're overriding your clinical judgment without seeing the patient is the core problem.

Have you started the appeal process? There are a few things that can strengthen it.

I have appealed, yes. I wrote a detailed narrative with the clinical findings, attached the radiographs, the probing chart, everything. My office manager spent two hours on this. Two hours of her time that I am paying for, to argue with people who have no clinical training about whether my patient deserves treatment.

That's the hidden cost that never gets talked about — the administrative burden on your practice. Hours of staff time, your time writing narratives, all uncompensated. And meanwhile your patient is waiting in discomfort.

A few things that sometimes help push appeals through:

1. **Cite the AAP classification system** explicitly — link their findings to the 2017 staging and grading framework. Reviewers sometimes respond better to formal diagnostic codes.
2. **Include a periodontal disease progression statement** — explain what happens if treatment is delayed. Tooth loss, systemic health connections.
3. **Request a peer-to-peer review** — ask that your appeal be reviewed by a licensed periodontist, not a general reviewer. You have that right in most states.

The peer-to-peer review is an excellent suggestion. I will request that. Let them put a periodontist on the phone with me and I will walk them through every millimeter of this case. What bothers me most, jan, is that the patient suffers. She called my office today, confused. She does not understand why her insurance will not cover a treatment her doctor says she needs. What am I supposed to tell her?

That's the worst part — the patient is caught in the middle and feels like something is wrong with them or their claim. Here's what I'd suggest telling her:

- Reassure her that this is an insurance issue, not a clinical one. Her condition absolutely requires treatment.
- Let her know you're actively appealing and fighting for her coverage.
- If the appeal fails, give her options — a payment plan for out-of-pocket costs, or a letter she can send to her employer's HR about the inadequacy of the dental plan.

Sometimes patients contacting their own employer's benefits department creates pressure from the other side. Insurance companies don't like angry employer clients.

That is a very good point about the employer angle. I had not considered that. You know, the fundamental problem is that dental insurance was designed in the 1960s and the annual maximum has barely changed since then. Most plans still cap at $1,500 per year. Adjusted for inflation, that number should be over $10,000. The entire model is broken.

You're absolutely right. The $1,500 annual maximum has been essentially frozen for decades while every cost in dentistry — materials, technology, lab fees, overhead — has skyrocketed. It's one of the biggest structural problems in dental care.

And the incentive structure is backwards. Insurance companies save money by denying preventive and early-intervention treatments, but then they pay far more later when the patient needs extractions, implants, or dentures. It's penny-wise, pound-foolish.

Some practices have started moving toward membership or subscription models for uninsured patients as an alternative. Have you considered anything like that?

I have thought about it. Several colleagues have implemented in-house membership plans for their patients. It is appealing because it removes the insurance company from the equation entirely. But the reality is that most of my patients have insurance through their employers and they expect to use it. I cannot simply tell them to ignore their benefits. It is a difficult situation, jan. I became a clinician to treat patients, not to argue with bureaucrats.

That frustration is completely valid, and it's shared by a lot of practitioners. You went through years of rigorous training to make clinical decisions, and having those decisions second-guessed by non-clinicians is demoralizing.

For now, I'd say: push hard on the peer-to-peer review, document everything meticulously for future claims with this insurer, and maybe have your office manager track how many hours per month are spent on appeals. That data itself can be powerful — both for internal decisions about which insurance panels to stay on, and for advocacy with professional organizations pushing for reform.

That is excellent advice. Tracking the administrative hours is something I should have been doing from the start. I will have my office manager begin logging that immediately. Thank you for letting me vent, jan. I feel somewhat better. Now I need to go prepare for my afternoon patients. At least in the operatory, I am in control.

Ha — that's a great way to look at it. In the chair, your expertise is what matters. I hope the peer-to-peer review goes your way, and that your patient gets the treatment she needs. Good luck this afternoon.
